summary: McKenzie shares an example of proactive customer service email he sent when a customer's credit card had issues with Google Checkout, resulting in triple charges. He canceled the duplicate orders and delivered the software immediately on trust, rather than waiting for payment resolution. He then critiques his own email, noting flaws in its language and tone. The post advocates for taking control of customer interactions when circumstances go wrong, rather than letting process failures damage the customer relationship.
key_points: When payment processing fails, proactively resolve the situation in the customer's favor rather than waiting for the system to sort itself out
Conditional apologies are worse than no apology — they signal you're trying to avoid fault rather than fix the problem
Shop-talk and jargon should be eliminated from customer communications in favor of plain language
When circumstances beyond your control make a customer interaction imperfect, take control of the circumstances yourself
money_quotes: Heck yes I am fanatical about customer service. Are you?
When circumstances beyond your control make your customer interaction less than absolutely perfect, control the freaking circumstances.
Clumsy is forgivable but conditional apologies are the worst thing ever — they make it seem like you're trying to avoid fault.
I'm going to trust that Google will resolve it to our mutual satisfaction, and deliver the software to you now.
If Google cannot resolve the issue and we are forced to cancel the order, feel free to keep the software, and accept my apologies for the inconvenience.
I love Checkout but I love my customers more.
key_insight: Great customer service means proactively resolving problems in the customer's favor and controlling the narrative, even when the failure is someone else's fault.
